summary:
The Warring States Period (475–221 BC) was the final and most intense phase of Zhou dynasty fragmentation, when seven major states — Qin, Chu, Qi, Yan, Han, Wei, and Zhao — engaged in near-continuous warfare, military innovation, and political transformation that ultimately produced a unified Chinese empire. The period saw the mass armies of peasant conscripts replace the aristocratic chariot-based warfare of earlier eras, the development of iron weapons and cavalry, and the systematic development of military strategy recorded in texts like [[event:1115|Sun Tzu and Art of War]]'s The Art of War.

The period was simultaneously one of explosive intellectual creativity — the [[event:1112|Hundred Schools of Thought]] flowered as competing states patronized thinkers offering solutions to political survival and administrative efficiency. Confucian, Daoist, Legalist, Mohist, and other schools all emerged or developed in this period of crisis. States competed not just militarily but administratively: those that adopted the most efficient bureaucratic and taxation systems, like Qin under its Legalist reforms (Lord Shang's reforms), grew strongest. The period ended with Qin's final conquests (230–221 BC) under [[event:3141|Qin Shi Huang]], who unified all seven states into the first Chinese empire. The century-and-a-half of interstate competition produced both the trauma of massive warfare and the institutional and intellectual innovations that would define Chinese civilization.
